title: Treatment of Typhoid Fever with Cefamandole
abstract: Cefamandole therapy was evaluated in nine patients with typhoid fever. Six patients, including alH five who received the antibiotic by continuous intravenous drip (8.0 g daily), were cured. Dosage schediles resulting in maintenance of antibiotic concentrations in serum high above the MIC seemed to correlate well with treatment success.
text: Cefamandole therapy was evaluated in nine patients with typhoid fever. Six patients, including alH five who received the antibiotic by continuous intravenous drip (8.0 g daily), were cured. Dosage schediles resulting in maintenance of antibiotic concentrations in serum high above the MIC seemed to correlate well with treatment success.
main_text: Reports on the potential usefulness of cephalosporin anti- biotic in the treatment of salmonella bacteremia have been scanty and inconclusive (3). Cefazolin, which we found previously to be effective in acute enteric fever when admin- istered in a daily dose of 3.0 to 6.0 g for 11 to 16 days (7), has not been further evaluated by other investigators. Cefaman- dole, in a dose of 2 g intravenously (i.v.) every 6 h for 14 days, proved curative in 1 patient with typhoid fever (4); its use, however, in a dosage schedule of 60 to 240 mg/kg daily led to therapeutic failures in 7 of 19 patients with salmonella bacteremia (1) . The results of the latter study should be interpreted with caution because the majority of patients had underlying hepatosplenic schistosomiasis. Furthermore, variable dosage schedules were employed, and any possible relationship between a particular dosage regimen and treat- ment outcome was left uncertain. The present open trial was aimed at evaluating the thera- peutic response of the acute typhoidal illness to cefamandole in light of the in vitro activity of the antibiotic against the S. typhi isolates and its attainable levels in serum during treatment. Nine patients, five males and four females, rang- ing in age, between 16 and 40 years, were studied. In every case, the clinical picture was compatible with typhoid fever and the diagnosis was confirmed by isolating S. typhi from blood cultures. Bacteriological identification of the isolates was based on API 20E system (Analytab Products) and slide agglutination tests. None of the patients had a major compli- cation, another underlying disease, or laboratory evidence of impaired renal function. Susceptibility of the isolates to cefamandole was determined by the serial twofold dilution technique on Mueller-Hinton agar (BBL Microbiology Systems), using the replicating apparatus described by Steers et al. (5) . The final inoculum delivered on each spot of the agar surface contained ca. 104 CFU. The MIC was defined as the lowest antibiotic concentration inhibiting visible surface growth, but a faint haze or a single colony was disregarded. Cefamandole nafate (Eli Lilly & Co.) was administered to five patients by continuous i.v. drip in a total dose of 8.0 g daily. The other four patients received the antibiotic inter- mittently by short i.v. infusions containing 1.0 g, every 4 h in three patients and every 3 h in one patient (Table 1 ). During treatment, blood samples were collected for antibiotic assay. For the patients receiving intermittent therapy, the samnples were collected immediately before an antibiotic dose was * Corresponding author. administered to monitor the trough cefamandole concentra- tions in serum. Sampling from the patients receiving treat- ment by continuous i.v. drip followed an arbitrary schedule, as no specific timing was considered necessary. Cefaman- dole assay was carried out by the conventional cylinder- plate agar diffusion technique, with Bacillus subtilis ATCC 6633 spore suspension (Difco Laboratories) serving as the test species in accordance with a procedure previously described (8) . The nine S. typhi isolates were susceptible to cefamandole (as well as to chloramphenicol, cotrimoxazole, and ampicillin). Cefamandole MICs were in the range of 0.125 to 0.5 4g/ ml (Table 1 ). All five patients who received the antibiotic by continuous i.v. drip responded satisfactorily to treatment. Early symptoms and signs of improvement, including de- cline of fever, were noted within 3 to 5 days of therapy, and complete defervescence was confirmed after 3 to 9 days (Table 1 ). Treatment was continued for a total of 10 to 15 days without any apparent adverse effects, except for phlebi- tis at the site of the i.v. line that occurred in three patients and may have contributed to delayed defervescence. Three patients were followed up for a period of at least 4 weeks and none relapsed. The other two patients were discharged from the hospital afebrile and asymptomatic, but they were not available at a later date for evaluation. In contrast, cefamandole failed to control infection in three of the four patients who received intermittent i.v. therapy (Table 1 ). The acute symptoms persisted in severity and fever continued unabated. The absence of any signs of a therapeutic response after 5 to 7 days of treatment was an indication to replace cefamandole with another therapeutic agent and to consider the case as a treatment failure. Ultimately, all patients recovered without any complications or adverse effects. Despite the obvious limitations imposed by the design and size of this study, the results clearly point to the dosage schedule as an important variable in assessing therapeutic agents in enteric fever. It has been previously postulated that successful control of typhoid infection may not depend only on the attainable peak levels of the drug in serum as related to the MIC against the infecting organism but also on how well the inhibitory concentrations are sustained in between peak values (6) . Considering the individual patients de- scribed in this trial, the arbitrary ratio of the mean trough (or presumably sustained) antibiotic concentration in serum to the MIC against the infecting organism (Table 1 ) provided a quotient that correlated well with the treatment response. Although similarly derived, this quotient is different from the "inhibitory quotient" as defined by Ellner and Neu (2) in that actually attained trough antibiotic concentrations were Treatment dupa-Wt dfailydose Mmandole Cefamandole ment ration (days)   tient(kg) (g) and concn in MIC (>eg/ml) out-before com- no. used rather than the expected peak drug level. The calculat- ed quotient values exceeded 20 in four patients (patients 1, 2, 4, and 5) who were all cured, but they were less than 5 in two patients (patient 6 and 7) who were both considered to be treatment failures. The three other patients had calculated quotient values in the range of 8 to 12. Two of these patients were cured, and the third failed to respond to treatment. These observations lend further support to the view that the therapeutic potential of a drug in enteric fever is better explored by using a dosage schedule which would allow maintenance of drug levels in serum high above the MICs for the longest time intervals possible. In view of the wide range of permissible dosage schedules for new antimicrobial agents with promising in vitro activity against salmonellae, some controversial issues are best avoided if reference is made to treatment regimens rather than to the drugs per se in any conclusions pertaining to assessment or comparative evalua- tion of their therapeutic efficacy.
